title: 37-Key 555 Polyphonic Synthesizer
alt: 37-key polyphonic synthesizer built from salvaged keybed and 555 timer oscillators
description: A fully analog polyphonic synthesizer built from scratch. 37 keys, 12 hand-tuned 555 oscillators, 7 octaves via CD4024 counters, onboard fuzz, PT2399 delay, transformer saturation, and white noise on every key. Designed and built entirely by me as a learning project — and a real instrument.
bullets: 37 semi-weighted keys salvaged from an M-Audio Axiom 61
12 × 555 timer oscillators, each tuned with a trim pot (one full chromatic octave)
CD4024 binary counter per oscillator — 7 octaves each, 84 tones total
Squarewave output + low-pass filtered 'sine-ish' output with blend knob
White noise generator on every key via shared membrane switch rail
LM386 amplification throughout — no split supply needed, runs on 9V
LFO vibrato via 555 monostable circuit on each oscillator
Fuzz stage: LM386-based with power sag control for weirder tones
PT2399 delay module (dual chip) with depth and rate controls
Transformer saturation stage — audio driven backwards through an AC transformer

steps.title: Ideation — Designing the Instrument
steps.text:
Started by mapping out what I actually wanted to build: a 37-note polyphonic analog synth, designed and wired entirely by hand. The signal chain I landed on runs from the keybed through tone generation, mixing, and a full effects chain before hitting the output stage.

Each key triggers both a squarewave oscillator signal and a white noise source. The oscillator output splits into a raw squarewave path and a filtered 'sine-ish' path — blended with a knob. Effects chain: fuzz (LM386-based with power sag), PT2399 delay, transformer saturation, then a final output amp with passive compression.
steps.alt: Signal chain diagram for the 555 polyphonic synth

steps.title: Step 1 — Salvaging the Keybed
steps.text:
Pulled the keybed from a broken M-Audio Axiom 61 — the MIDI calibration had died and a few keys were cracked, but the keybed mechanism itself was solid. Semi-weighted keys, decent feel.

Used a Dremel to cut out the usable 37-key section. The tricky part would come next: modifying the membrane switch PCB underneath without destroying the traces.

steps.title: Step 2 — Modifying the Membrane Switches
steps.text:
The membrane switch PCB needed serious surgery. Cut all the traces to separate the top and bottom button contacts, then tied all the bottom contacts together into a single rail — that becomes the white noise bus, playing on every key.

For each individual note, I scraped back the PCB mask to expose the copper trace and soldered a wire to each side of the button. This was the most frustrating part of the whole build. Too much movement and the trace lifts right off. Ended up making a 12-wire harness first, zip-tying it to the board, then soldering — much easier than fighting loose wires.

Where traces got damaged, I verified continuity and superglued the wire down to lock it in place. Labeled every wire (C1, C#1, D1…) and checked everything with a multimeter before moving on. Wiring resistance came in over 100k ohms — which actually works in my favor, since the passive mixer uses 100k resistors anyway. Just needs an amp stage to recover the level.
steps.alt: Membrane switch PCB with modified traces and wiring harness

steps.title: Step 3 — Amplification
steps.text: Chose the LM386 as the main amplifier throughout the project. The reason is simple: it runs on a single positive supply. The whole synth is powered from 9V, so I don't want to deal with split rails. The LM386 handles amp duties at the mixer output, the sine filter output, and the white noise channel.

steps.title: Step 4 — Tone Generator
steps.text:
The tone generator is the heart of the instrument. 12 × 555 timers, each configured as a squarewave oscillator — one per semitone, covering a full chromatic octave. Each has its own RC values and a trim pot for fine tuning.

Each 555 feeds into a CD4024 binary ripple counter, which divides the frequency down through 7 binary stages — giving 7 octaves per oscillator, 84 total pitches from a single board. To tune the synth, you only need to tune 12 oscillators. The 37 notes for the keybed are selected from those 84 available outputs.
steps.alt: 555 timer oscillator board with CD4024 binary counters

steps.title: Step 5 — The Mixer
steps.text:
The mixer takes all 37 squarewave signals through a passive resistor network, then splits into two paths: raw squarewave and a low-pass filtered output run through 4 cascaded filter stages and re-amplified to match level. These two outputs go either side of a blend knob.

I wanted to filter each note individually for a true polyphonic sine, but the component count made that impractical. Running the summed output through the filter instead means it's more of a 'filtered output' than a true sine — but it should still sound interesting and very different from the raw square.

The mixer board also handles white noise (its own LM386 amp channel) and a line-in jack. External controls: Oscillator Blend, Oscillator Volume, White Noise Volume, Line In Volume.
steps.alt: Mixer board with passive summing network and filter stages

steps.title: Step 6 — Effects Chain
steps.text:
Three effect stages after the mixer:

1. Fuzz — LM386-based fuzz circuit with a 'power sag' feature that starves the supply and produces unusual, collapsing tones. Controls: bypass switch, power sag knob, gain knob.

2. PT2399 Delay — dual PT2399 chip delay module for longer delay times and more headroom. Controls: depth, rate.

3. Transformer Saturation — audio signal driven backwards through a small AC transformer. The transformer charges and clips the signal on the way through, adding vintage harmonic saturation. Controls: sat bypass switch, input level, output level.

Also planned: a 555 monostable LFO circuit connected to each oscillator for vibrato effect.
steps.alt: Effects chain boards: fuzz, delay, transformer saturation

steps.title: Step 7 — Output Stage
steps.text: Final stage: a small amplifier circuit with volume and tone controls, followed by a passive compressor to tame peaks before hitting the 1/4" output jack. The passive compressor keeps things from getting too spiky when playing lots of notes at once.
steps.alt: Output stage with passive compressor and 1/4 inch jack
